humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLMs on repairing OCR errors in 19th-century British newspaper articles containing historical vocabulary, old currency systems, and archaic spelling conventions.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Text-only post-OCR correction task mapping corrupted historical text to human-transcribed ground truth segments.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Denoising of textual representations to correct OCR mistakes (character substitutions, insertions, deletions) while maintaining historical linguistic context and named entities.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: The task is specialized for historical text post-OCR correction rather than general-purpose multi-task LLM evaluation.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary objective is to evaluate generative LLMs (Llama 2) for post-OCR correction, comparing them against BART.

arts_humanities_data.note: Evaluates on historical British newspapers (History, Language and Linguistics ASJC areas).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses Character Error Rate (CER) reduction computed via Levenshtein distance.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published in May 2024 (LT4HALA 2024 workshop at LREC-COLING 2024).

english_language.note: The paper and the BLN600 benchmark dataset are in English.

summary: This paper investigates the adaptation of generative Large Language Models (LLMs) for post-OCR correction of historical documents, focusing on 19th-century British newspapers. By instruction-tuning Llama 2 and comparing it to fine-tuned BART on the BLN600 corpus, the authors demonstrate that a prompt-based generative approach significantly improves OCR quality and adapts exceptionally well to low-data environments.
key_findings: Instruction-tuned Llama 2 (13B) achieved a 54.51% average reduction in Character Error Rate (CER), significantly outperforming the fine-tuned BART-large model (23.30% reduction). Furthermore, Llama 2 demonstrated robust performance even when trained on extremely small subsets of data (down to 80 samples).
